title: Caraway rye bread
subtitle: A dense mixed-grain loaf with caraway seeds throughout
excerpt: One loaf made from equal parts rye flour and strong white flour with caraway seeds through the dough. Denser and more flavourful than a white loaf, it slices well and keeps for four days. Particularly good with cold meats, aged cheese, or smoked fish.
sourceNotes: Mixed rye and wheat loaves with caraway are documented throughout Central and Northern European cookery from at least the seventeenth century. Recipes appear in pre-1928 German and Austrian household cookbooks, including Katharina Prato, Die Suddeutsche Kuche (1901 edition, public domain). The caraway rye form is also well-attested in pre-1928 Scandinavian and Austro-Hungarian domestic baking literature available through digitised Central European library archives.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes or toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dough can be refrigerated after shaping overnight and baked the following day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 210°C. The loaf needs a full bake to set the dense rye crumb.

glossaryTerms.definition: Rye flour contains gluten proteins but they form a much weaker, more extensible network than wheat gluten. A dough made entirely from rye is sticky and tears rather than stretches, and it will not pass the windowpane test. Mixing rye with strong white flour, as in this recipe, gives the loaf enough structure to rise in a tin while keeping the flavour and density of a rye bread. The more rye in the blend, the denser and more close-crumbed the result.

content.text: This is a Mischbrot-style loaf: half rye flour, half strong white flour, with caraway seeds through the dough.
is too weak to support a freeform loaf, so this one bakes in a tin. The caraway gives the bread its characteristic anise note. One 900 g tin loaf, ready in about 3 hours.

Combine both flours, the yeast, salt, and caraway seeds in a large bowl. Dissolve the treacle in the warm water if using, then pour into the flour and mix until a dough comes together. Knead for 10 minutes by hand or 8 minutes by machine. The dough will be stickier and less elastic than a pure white dough; this is normal. Add no more flour unless it is impossible to work with.
Cover the bowl and leave in a warm place for about 90 minutes. The dough will rise but may not fully double, which is normal for a high-rye blend.

Grease the loaf tin and line the base with baking paper. With wet hands, shape the dough into an oval and lower it into the tin seam-side down. Smooth the top. Cover loosely and leave for 60 minutes until the dough has risen to near the rim of the tin. Heat the oven to 200°C fan (210°C conventional). Bake for 40 to 45 minutes, until the loaf is dark brown, has pulled away from the sides, and sounds hollow when tapped on the base. Cool on a wire rack before slicing.
